---
title: "*Where Does Sherwood Hide His Unit?* and *What the Schmidt?* (Dimensionless Numbers Principles and Examples) Reference Page"
author: "DOFPro group"
format: html
html-math-method: mathjax
---
::: {.callout-important title="Still Under Construction!"}
This web page is just a bare outline. The complete details have not been filled in yet.
:::
# Intro to *Main Title*

Dimensionless Numbers explanation

## Where Does Sherwood Hide His Unit? Principles

Video 1 explanation

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/A348vNv5t9c?si=ea8avmzq6pTLtmas" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
[Visuals](../../NotYetHere.qmd)

## What the Schmidt? Examples

Video  explanation

<iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/74SD0k_qBc8?si=JSO94bTp2EA3DFIO" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>
[Visuals](../../NotYetHere.qmd)

# Examples and Definitions

## Examples Links

## Definitions

Dimensionless Numbers or Dimensionless Parameters
: A dimensionless number is a quantity that has no physical units and is used to characterize the behavior of a system or process. These numbers are often ratios of physical quantities and are used to simplify complex phenomena by reducing the number of variables involved. Examples include the Reynolds number, Prandtl number, and Sherwood number, which are commonly used in fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and mass transfer, respectively.

Dimensional Homogeneity
: A principle in physics and engineering that states that all terms in a physical equation must have the same dimensions. This means that the units on both sides of an equation must be consistent, ensuring that the equation is physically meaningful. Dimensional homogeneity is a fundamental requirement for the validity of any physical equation and is often used as a check for the correctness of derived equations.

# Other Links and Videos

## Other Links

## Previous and Following Videos
